steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question asking which finding would most directly support Mulder and Spellman's conclusion: that invasive species are advantaged by delays in the onset of subfreezing autumn temperatures. A supporting finding must show the invasive outperforming the native specifically because of that delayed onset.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although *C. canadensis* and *P. aviculare* both tended to produce leaves later into autumn in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, the extension was much greater for *P. aviculare* than for *C. canadensis*.: Both species benefit from the delay, but the invasive knotgrass (P. aviculare) extends its leaf-producing period far more than the native dogwood (C. canadensis). This directly shows the invasive gaining a larger advantage from the delayed subfreezing onset — exactly the researchers' conclusion. ✓
  • Choice B — Although *C. canadensis* and *P. aviculare* both tended to produce more leaves overall in years with late subfreezing temperature onset than they did in years with historically typical temperature patterns, the years with late subfreezing temperature onset also had early growing season onset in spring.: Any additional leaf production is attributed partly to spring onset as well, muddying the causal link to autumn delays alone. This does not cleanly support the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — Although *C. canadensis* and *P. aviculare* tended to stop producing leaves at about the same time in years with historically typical temperature patterns, *P. aviculare* stopped producing leaves sooner than *C. canadensis* did in years with late subfreezing temperature onset.: This reverses the expected result — it shows the invasive doing worse, not better, when subfreezing onset is delayed. This contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — Although significant interannual variations in subfreezing temperature onset were observed during the study, neither *P. aviculare* nor *C. canadensis* showed any significant interannual variation in the cessation of leaf production.: If neither species responds to the variation in subfreezing onset, there is no differential advantage for the invasive. This undermines the conclusion entirely. ✗
A is correct.
